h1Title: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic emergency in which intraocular pressure rises suddenly and severely — typically to 40-70 mmHg versus the normal 10-21 mmHg — when the iris physically blocks aqueous humour outflow through the trabecular meshwork. It affects roughly 0.5-1% of adults over age 50 globally and accounts for nearly half of all blindness from glaucoma in Asia. The classic presentation is a sudden unilateral red, painful eye with blurred vision, haloes around lights, nausea, and vomiting. Every hour of untreated elevated pressure destroys retinal ganglion cells permanently; irreversible vision loss can occur within hours. Emergency treatment with pressure-lowering drops and laser iridotomy is sight-saving and highly effective when initiated promptly.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 20 million people worldwide have angle-closure glaucoma; 4 million are bilaterally blind from it (Tham et al. Ophthalmology 2014)
keyStats.demographics: Women are 2-4 times more affected than men; East Asian populations have 3-5 times higher prevalence than Europeans; Inuit populations carry the highest known prevalence globally
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence age 55-70; rare under 40 except in association with lens-induced or secondary causes
keyStats.globalCases: Angle-closure glaucoma causes approximately 4 million cases of bilateral blindness — about 50% of all glaucoma blindness worldwide (Quigley and Broman 2006)

definition: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (ICD-10: H40.21) is a form of glaucoma in which the anterior chamber drainage angle closes abruptly, blocking aqueous humour outflow and producing a sudden, symptomatic, and severe rise in intraocular pressure (IOP). The mechanism in most cases is pupillary block: aqueous accumulates behind the iris, bowing it anteriorly (iris bombe), until contact with the trabecular meshwork obstructs the angle. Less commonly, plateau iris configuration or lens-induced causes drive the closure. The acute form differs fundamentally from chronic angle-closure glaucoma, in which angle closure develops gradually and asymptomatically over months to years. Acute attacks are almost always unilateral, though the fellow eye has narrow angles and a greater than 50% lifetime risk of a future attack. The condition falls within the broader glaucoma spectrum — a family of optic neuropathies characterised by progressive loss of retinal ganglion cells and their axons — but is specifically defined by its angle-closure mechanism and its acute, potentially blinding time course. Acute angle-closure glaucoma is managed by ophthalmologists as an emergency, and laser or surgical iridotomy is the definitive treatment to open the blocked angle.

typesClassification.description: The most common mechanism. Relative pupillary block traps aqueous posterior to the iris, causing forward iris bowing and angle closure. Precipitated by mydriasis (low light, dilating drops) in anatomically predisposed eyes with shallow anterior chambers.

Peripheral iris configuration causes angle closure even after iridotomy relieves pupillary block. The ciliary body positions the iris periphery anteriorly. Definitive management is laser iridoplasty or surgical peripheral iridectomy.
An enlarged cataractous lens pushes the iris anteriorly, closing the angle. Common in older patients with advanced cataract. Lens extraction resolves the problem permanently.

Caused by neovascular glaucoma (rubeosis iridis blocking the angle), inflammatory synechiae (posterior synechiae from uveitis), or malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection posteriorly). These have distinct management pathways from primary angle closure.

primarySymptoms: Sudden severe unilateral eye pain, often described as a deep ache or pressure behind the eye, typically starting in the evening when ambient light levels fall and the pupil naturally dilates.
Acutely blurred vision in the affected eye, ranging from mild haziness to near-total loss of vision, caused by corneal oedema from elevated intraocular pressure.
Coloured haloes or rainbow circles around lights, produced by light refraction through a oedematous cornea, often the earliest visual symptom preceding frank pain.
A fixed, mid-dilated pupil (approximately 4-6 mm) that reacts poorly or not at all to light — a reliable examination finding in an established acute attack.
Ciliary injection: a circumcorneal ring of dilated episcleral vessels giving the eye a characteristically 'red ring' appearance around the limbus, distinct from conjunctival infection.
Nausea and vomiting, caused by a vagally mediated response to severe ocular pain, present in 30-40% of cases and sometimes distracting from the ocular diagnosis.
Headache on the same side as the affected eye, often severe and sometimes mimicking migraine or cluster headache, leading to misdiagnosis and delayed treatment.
Firm 'rock-hard' eyeball on gentle palpation through the closed lid, reflecting extremely elevated intraocular pressure — a clinical sign valuable when tonometry is unavailable.
Corneal oedema seen on slit-lamp examination as a 'steamy' or hazy cornea, preventing clear fundal view and confirming clinically significant pressure elevation.
earlyWarningSigns: Subacute or intermittent episodes of blurred vision with haloes around lights at night that resolve spontaneously within hours — called 'glaucomflecken episodes', indicating recurrent partial angle closure.
Previous ophthalmological finding of a shallow anterior chamber or narrow drainage angle on gonioscopy, flagging anatomical predisposition.
Fellow-eye acute attack history — after one eye has an acute attack, the contralateral eye has a lifetime risk exceeding 50% of a similar event.
Dilating eye drops causing eye pain or blurred vision — an adverse reaction reflecting the eye's susceptibility to angle closure under pharmacological mydriasis.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe eye pain with acute visual loss, mid-dilated non-reactive pupil, and red eye — ophthalmological emergency requiring immediate presentation to an emergency eye unit or accident and emergency department.

Nausea, vomiting, and headache combined with unilateral eye redness and blurred vision — this triad is classic for acute angle-closure glaucoma and must not be attributed to gastrointestinal disease without checking intraocular pressure.
Intraocular pressure above 40 mmHg on tonometry — confirms a sight-threatening pressure crisis requiring immediate treatment regardless of severity of symptoms.
Corneal oedema with a narrow or closed anterior chamber angle on gonioscopy or anterior segment OCT — confirms the diagnosis and triggers emergency treatment.

causes.cause: Anatomically narrow anterior chamber angle (primary predisposition)
causes.description: Short axial length hyperopic eyes, thick or anteriorly positioned lenses, and shallow anterior chambers create anatomical conditions in which the iris-lens interface generates relative pupillary block. This blocks aqueous flow from posterior to anterior chamber, bows the iris forward, and closes the trabecular meshwork. This is present in the vast majority of primary acute angle-closure cases.

causes.cause: Pupil dilation (mydriasis) in a predisposed eye
causes.description: Mid-dilation at approximately 4-6 mm creates maximum pupillary block because the iris is thick enough to press against the lens but not pulled enough centrally to open the channel. Dim lighting, topical mydriatic drops, anticholinergic medications (antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics), and sympathomimetics can all precipitate an attack in predisposed eyes.

The crystalline lens increases in thickness throughout adult life at approximately 0.02 mm per year, progressively narrowing the anterior chamber angle. Cataract formation further enlarges the lens (phacomorphic mechanism), physically pushing the iris anteriorly and precipitating or aggravating angle closure.

An anteriorly rotated ciliary body positions the peripheral iris against the trabecular meshwork even when axial length and lens position are normal. This anatomical variant is less common than pupillary block but explains why some patients develop angle closure recurrence after a technically successful laser iridotomy.

causes.cause: Secondary causes: neovascularisation, inflammation, and pharmacological
causes.description: Rubeosis iridis from diabetic retinopathy or retinal vein occlusion causes neovascular membranes to close the angle. Posterior synechiae from uveitis can cause 360-degree pupillary block (seclusio pupillae). Topiramate, sulfonamides, and certain antidepressants can cause choroidal effusion and anterior rotation of the lens-iris diaphragm, producing drug-induced angle closure.

riskFactors.factor: East Asian, Inuit, or South Asian ethnicity
riskFactors.description: East Asians have significantly higher rates of primary angle-closure glaucoma than Europeans; Inuit populations carry the highest prevalence globally. Shallower anterior chambers and more anterior lens positioning in these populations are the anatomical basis.

Women are 2-4 times more likely to develop acute angle-closure glaucoma than men of the same age, related to shorter axial eye length, more prominent lens convexity, and shallower anterior chambers on average.
Prevalence roughly doubles with each decade over 50 as the crystalline lens continues to enlarge with age. Cataract formation accelerates angle narrowing.
Hyperopic eyes are structurally shorter (lower axial length) with thicker, more anteriorly positioned lenses and shallower anterior chambers, making them anatomically predisposed to angle closure.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of acute angle-closure glaucoma
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of affected patients have 2-5 times higher risk of narrow angles and acute angle-closure attacks; heritability studies suggest polygenic contribution from loci controlling anterior segment development.

riskFactors.factor: Use of anticholinergic or sympathomimetic medications
riskFactors.description: Topical mydriatics, tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, inhaled anticholinergics, and topiramate can precipitate acute attacks in anatomically predisposed eyes. Ophthalmological review before starting these agents is appropriate in high-risk individuals.
Dense, swollen cataracts physically narrow the angle and should prompt early lens extraction in eyes showing progressive angle narrowing on gonioscopy.

affectedDemographics: East Asian populations (Chinese, Korean, Japanese, Vietnamese) bear roughly 87% of the global burden of angle-closure glaucoma blindness (Tham et al. 2014), with prevalence rates 3-5 times higher than in Europeans.
Women account for approximately 60-70% of acute angle-closure episodes, driven by shorter axial eye length and deeper anterior-to-posterior lens positioning.
Adults over age 60 carry the highest absolute incidence because of progressive lens enlargement and iris stiffening with reduced sphincter efficiency.
Inuit populations in Canada, Alaska, and Greenland have the highest documented prevalence rates of any ethnic group, approaching 2-3% of adults over age 40.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is clinical and urgent. History establishes the onset (acute hours vs subacute days), symptom character (pain, halos, visual loss, nausea), medications, and any previous episodes. Examination documents visual acuity, pupil responses, and slit-lamp findings. The core diagnostic triad is elevated intraocular pressure on tonometry (typically 40-70 mmHg), corneal oedema on slit-lamp, and a closed or appositionally narrow anterior chamber angle confirmed by gonioscopy or anterior segment optical coherence tomography (AS-OCT). Goldmann applanation tonometry is the standard method for IOP measurement; non-contact tonometry is acceptable as a screening tool when applanation is unavailable. Gonioscopy — in which a mirrored contact lens applied to the anaesthetised cornea allows direct view of the drainage angle — remains the definitive test for angle status and identifies the trabecular meshwork, Schwalbe's line, and any peripheral anterior synechiae. AS-OCT provides non-contact assessment of angle architecture and is particularly useful for documenting angle status before and after laser iridotomy. Ophthalmoscopy assesses the optic disc for cupping and pallor; fundal view may be limited by corneal oedema and a dilated pupil. Perimetry (visual field testing) is deferred from the acute visit but performed within weeks to baseline optic nerve function. The differential includes acute iritis (no corneal oedema, normal pressure, intense photophobia), cluster headache (normal eye examination), and optic neuritis (no pressure elevation, pain on eye movement).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures intraocular pressure; IOP over 40 mmHg in the context of symptoms confirms a pressure crisis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescein eye drops and a blue light source; a gentle probe touches the anaesthetised cornea for seconds. Painless; result immediate. The normal range is 10-21 mmHg; acute angle closure typically produces 40-70 mmHg.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses corneal oedema, anterior chamber depth and cells, iris configuration, and pupil state — all key diagnostic features
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sitting at an instrument with a chin rest and forehead strap; the ophthalmologist directs a focused beam of light across the eye. Takes 5-10 minutes. Steamy cornea, shallow anterior chamber, and mid-dilated pupil confirm the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the anterior chamber drainage angle; confirms whether the angle is open, narrow, or closed and detects peripheral anterior synechiae
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A mirrored contact lens is placed on the anaesthetised eye after a drop of gel. The ophthalmologist examines the angle via the slit lamp. Mild pressure sensation only; takes 5-10 minutes. Critical for confirming angle closure and planning treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-contact imaging of anterior chamber angle anatomy; quantifies angle-opening distance and documents iridotomy patency without touching the eye
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A camera scans the front of the eye from a distance of about 1 cm; patient stares at a fixation light. No contact or drops required. Results available immediately as cross-sectional images.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates optic nerve head cupping and pallor to gauge the degree of glaucomatous damage sustained before or during the attack
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The pupil is dilated with drops; the ophthalmologist examines the retina with a bright light. Dilation lasts 4-6 hours. View may be limited by corneal oedema initially; repeat examination after pressure is controlled is often needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline mapping of visual field defects from optic nerve damage; not performed in the acute crisis but essential for follow-up management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient presses a button whenever they see a flashing light in a white hemisphere. Takes 10-15 minutes per eye. Results map areas of irreversible optic nerve damage and guide long-term treatment decisions.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is a staged emergency-to-definitive protocol. In the acute phase, the goal is immediate and rapid IOP reduction to prevent further optic nerve and corneal damage. Medical treatment begins simultaneously with diagnosis: topical beta-blockers (timolol 0.5%), topical alpha-2 agonists (apraclonidine or brimonidine), and topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide) are instilled in rapid succession; systemic acetazolamide 500 mg IV or oral is added to reduce aqueous production systemically. In cases of persistent elevation above 50 mmHg, hyperosmotic agents (IV mannitol 1-2 g/kg over 30-45 minutes) rapidly dehydrate the vitreous and pull the lens-iris diaphragm posteriorly, opening the angle in emergency. Pilocarpine 1-2% (miotic) is added once IOP falls below 40 mmHg to constrict the pupil and physically pull the iris away from the angle; it has reduced efficacy at very high pressures due to ischemic iris sphincter paralysis. Analgesics and anti-emetics are provided for comfort. Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI): a focused Nd:YAG laser creates a full-thickness hole in the peripheral iris, allowing aqueous to bypass the pupillary block and equalise pressure between the posterior and anterior chambers. LPI is performed in the acute attack or within 24-48 hours after pressure is medically controlled. The fellow asymptomatic eye undergoes prophylactic LPI within days. European Glaucoma Society guidelines recommend LPI as the primary definitive procedure; surgical peripheral iridectomy is reserved for LPI failure, opaque corneas preventing laser, or plateau iris requiring iridoplasty. Lens extraction by phacoemulsification is increasingly used as both definitive treatment and prophylaxis in eyes where the lens contributes significantly to angle narrowing.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical beta-blocker (timolol 0.5%, one drop)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces aqueous humour production by approximately 25-30% by blocking beta-2 receptors in the ciliary epithelium. First agent instilled in the emergency protocol. Contraindicated in severe asthma, COPD, and heart block.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lowers IOP by 5-10 mmHg within 30-60 minutes; works synergistically with other agents

medicalTreatments.name: Topical alpha-2 agonist (apraclonidine 1% or brimonidine 0.1-0.2%, one drop)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces aqueous production and increases uveoscleral outflow. Applied immediately after the beta-blocker. Additive to beta-blockade and carbonic anhydrase inhibition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces IOP by 4-8 mmHg; combination of beta-blocker and alpha agonist typically lowers IOP by 30-40% within 2 hours

medicalTreatments.name: IV acetazolamide 500 mg (or oral if IV unavailable)
medicalTreatments.description: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that rapidly reduces aqueous production by 30-40%. Given systemically for faster and more complete effect than topical formulations in acute crisis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: IOP reduction of 10-20 mmHg within 1-2 hours; corneal oedema begins clearing, improving laser access

medicalTreatments.name: IV mannitol 1-2 g/kg over 30-45 minutes
medicalTreatments.description: Osmotic agent that dehydrates the vitreous body, pulling the lens-iris diaphragm posteriorly. Used when IOP remains above 50 mmHg despite topical and systemic agents, or when corneal oedema must be cleared urgently for laser treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Can reduce IOP by 20-30 mmHg within 30-60 minutes; particularly effective in lens-induced and malignant glaucoma mechanisms

medicalTreatments.name: Laser peripheral iridotomy (Nd:YAG laser)
medicalTreatments.description: Creates a full-thickness hole in the peripheral iris at the 10-12 o'clock position, eliminating relative pupillary block and equalising anterior and posterior chamber pressures. Definitive treatment for primary angle closure from pupillary block.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Breaks the acute attack in over 90% of primary pupillary block cases; lifetime prophylaxis of recurrence in successfully treated eyes; fellow-eye prophylactic LPI reduces contralateral attack risk from over 50% to under 5%

medicalTreatments.name: Pilocarpine 1-2% (miotic drops)
medicalTreatments.description: Constricts the pupil, pulling the iris periphery away from the trabecular meshwork. Efficacy is limited at IOP above 40 mmHg due to ischaemic iris sphincter paralysis; instilled after initial pressure lowering to assist in opening the angle before laser.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Facilitates angle opening when IOP is below 40 mmHg; reduces need for emergency surgical intervention in about 60% of cases when combined with the above agents

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical peripheral iridectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of a small piece of peripheral iris under local or general anaesthesia, creating an opening equivalent to laser iridotomy. Reserved for cases where corneal oedema or patient cooperation prevents laser treatment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relieves pupillary block in over 95% of primary angle-closure cases; carries slightly higher risk than LPI including bleeding, infection, and cataract acceleration

surgicalOptions.name: Phacoemulsification cataract extraction
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the crystalline lens deepens the anterior chamber by 1-2 mm and widens the angle substantially. Increasingly used as primary definitive treatment when a large lens contributes to angle closure, particularly in patients already requiring cataract removal.
surgicalOptions.successRate: The EAGLE trial (2016) showed lens extraction superior to LPI for IOP control and quality of life in primary angle-closure patients with IOP over 30 mmHg or with visual field defects

surgicalOptions.name: Trabeculectomy or tube shunt surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Filtering procedures that create a new drainage pathway for aqueous; reserved for cases where IOP remains uncontrolled despite LPI and medical therapy, or where peripheral anterior synechiae have permanently damaged the angle.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Target IOP of under 18 mmHg achieved in 70-85% of cases at 5 years; significant risk of complications including hypotony, bleb failure, and cataract acceleration

alternativeTreatments.name: Laser peripheral iridoplasty (ALPI)
alternativeTreatments.description: Applied to the peripheral iris to contract iris stroma and physically open the angle; used when pupillary block persists after iridotomy (plateau iris) or as a temporary measure to open the angle before definitive iridotomy. Evidence supports its use in plateau iris confirmed on AS-OCT (Ritch and Tham, Ophthalmology 2004).

whySeeSpecialist: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic emergency requiring same-day evaluation by an ophthalmologist with access to tonometry, slit-lamp, gonioscopy, and a laser or operating theatre. General practitioners and emergency physicians should refer immediately without waiting for the next available outpatient slot. After the acute episode, ongoing management by a glaucoma subspecialist is needed.
doctorSelectionGuide: An ophthalmologist with glaucoma subspecialty training is ideal for definitive management. For the acute attack, any ophthalmologist with emergency eye unit access is appropriate. For fellow-eye prophylaxis, gonioscopy expertise and access to Nd:YAG laser are the key requirements. Ensure the clinic can perform AS-OCT for post-treatment monitoring.
hospitalCriteria: Emergency ophthalmology service with same-day access to applanation tonometry and slit-lamp
Nd:YAG laser available for emergency or next-day iridotomy
IV mannitol and acetazolamide on formulary for acute medical management
Access to gonioscopy or anterior segment OCT for angle assessment
Intraocular surgery capability for cases requiring lens extraction or trabeculectomy
keyFacilities: Emergency eye unit or acute ophthalmology service
Glaucoma subspecialty clinic with laser suite
Tertiary ophthalmology centre for complex secondary or refractory cases
preventionStrategies: Screen first-degree relatives of affected patients with gonioscopy or AS-OCT; prophylactic laser iridotomy in anatomically narrow angles prevents acute attacks.
Perform prophylactic LPI in the fellow eye within days of an acute unilateral attack — reduces lifetime contralateral attack risk from over 50% to under 5%.
Review medication lists for anticholinergic, sympathomimetic, and sulphonamide agents in any patient with a history of narrow angles or prior angle closure.
Encourage Asian women aged 50 and over to have regular ophthalmic examinations including angle assessment; population-based screening reduces attack incidence in high-risk groups.
Early cataract extraction in eyes with lens-induced angle narrowing prevents phacomorphic acute angle closure.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a wallet card or medical-alert notification identifying narrow-angle glaucoma status so that health professionals avoid prescribing pupil-dilating drugs without ophthalmological review.
Avoid prolonged time in very dark environments (cinemas, dark rooms) which may promote semi-dilation and trigger subacute attacks in un-treated narrow angles.
Attend all scheduled follow-up appointments for IOP monitoring and optic disc assessment after the acute episode.
Use all prescribed glaucoma eye drops consistently; missing doses allows IOP to rise and risks further optic nerve damage.
Report any return of halos around lights or eye discomfort immediately — subacute recurrences need prompt evaluation before escalating to another acute attack.

recommended: Adequate hydration — dehydration reduces efficacy of hyperosmotic agents and may exacerbate systemic side-effects of acetazolamide
Green leafy vegetables and foods rich in antioxidant vitamins C and E — observational data link higher dietary antioxidant intake with lower glaucoma progression risk
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish — potential neuroprotective effects on retinal ganglion cells in animal models
Low-to-moderate caffeine intake — very high caffeine consumption has been associated with modest short-term IOP elevation in some studies
avoid: Very large volumes of fluid consumed rapidly — drinking over 1 litre of water quickly can transiently elevate IOP by 3-4 mmHg
Excessive alcohol — acute alcohol consumption causes initial IOP reduction followed by rebound elevation; chronic use disrupts trabecular meshwork function
High-dose supplemental vitamin C without medical advice when on acetazolamide (both affect renal tubular transport)
Inversion exercises such as headstands if significant optic nerve damage has occurred, as inversion elevates IOP by 3-8 mmHg

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) lowers IOP by 2-5 mmHg during activity and may have lasting benefits on aqueous dynamics. Heavy resistance training and breath-holding (Valsalva) can transiently spike IOP and should be avoided in advanced glaucoma with significant optic nerve damage. No specific exercise restrictions apply after successful iridotomy in eyes with well-controlled IOP.
dailyManagement: Instil prescribed glaucoma drops at the same time each day; use a mirror and pull the lower lid down, aim the drop at the lower conjunctival sac, and close the eye for 60 seconds.
Monitor for blurred vision, haloes, or eye pain and seek same-day ophthalmological review if symptoms recur — these indicate possible IOP spike even after iridotomy.
Carry a list of medications to avoid (topical and systemic mydriatics, tricyclics, antihistamines) in any pharmacist or emergency consultation.
Attend IOP check at 4-6 weeks post-iridotomy and then 3-6 monthly as directed by the ophthalmologist.
Use reading glasses as prescribed — prolonged close work in dim light causes near-reflex miosis but can create pupil-block in borderline cases; adequate lighting mitigates this.
prognosis: With prompt treatment, outcomes are generally good. Intraocular pressure normalises after successful medical treatment and laser iridotomy in over 90% of primary pupillary-block cases. Visual acuity typically recovers to near-baseline within 24-48 hours as corneal oedema resolves. Optic nerve damage from the acute event is determined by peak IOP and duration of pressure elevation; attacks lasting under 24 hours with peak IOP below 60 mmHg usually produce minimal permanent loss. However, approximately 30-50% of patients develop chronic angle-closure glaucoma with gradual optic nerve progression after the acute episode, requiring ongoing glaucoma management. Peripheral anterior synechiae forming during the acute attack can permanently damage the drainage angle, leaving residual elevated IOP despite iridotomy in 30-40% of cases. The fellow eye has a greater than 50% lifetime risk of an acute attack without prophylactic LPI; after prophylaxis, this risk falls to under 5%. Long-term visual prognosis depends on how much optic nerve damage occurred before IOP was controlled.
recoveryTimeline: Corneal oedema clears within 24-48 hours of IOP normalisation. Headache and eye pain resolve within hours of adequate pressure reduction. Visual acuity returns to near-baseline within 1-3 days in most uncomplicated cases. Laser iridotomy is performed within the first 24-48 hours after IOP control. Fellow-eye iridotomy within 1 week. Long-term IOP monitoring and optic nerve assessment at 4-6 weeks, 3 months, and every 6 months thereafter.
complications: Permanent visual field loss from glaucomatous optic neuropathy — the extent depends on peak IOP, duration of the attack, and pre-existing optic nerve status. Even a single severe acute attack can produce dense field defects.
Corneal endothelial damage and reduced cell count from sustained IOP elevation — can cause long-term corneal decompensation and clouding requiring eventual corneal transplantation in severe cases.

Anterior subcapsular cataract (glaukomflecken) — small white opacities form in the anterior lens capsule from focal ischemia during the high-pressure event, visible permanently as a marker of past acute angle closure.
Chronic angle-closure glaucoma — up to 50% of patients develop ongoing elevated IOP from angle damage after the acute event, requiring lifelong medical or surgical glaucoma treatment.
Fellow-eye acute angle closure — without prophylactic iridotomy, the contralateral eye has a greater than 50% lifetime risk of an identical acute attack.

Malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection) — a rare but severe complication of surgical intervention in angle-closure eyes where aqueous accumulates posteriorly, pushing the lens-iris diaphragm forward and causing paradoxical IOP elevation.

supportResources.name: International Glaucoma Association
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient information, peer support, and helpline for people living with glaucoma including acute angle-closure.

supportResources.name: Glaucoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based foundation with patient guides on types of glaucoma, treatment options, and research updates.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Glaucoma
supportResources.description: Patient-facing summaries of angle-closure glaucoma, iridotomy, and glaucoma medications with AAO editorial oversight.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute iritis (uveitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute iritis causes a painful red eye with intense photophobia, a small irregular pupil (miosis), and cells and flare in the anterior chamber. Intraocular pressure is normal or low in early iritis, whereas acute angle-closure produces high IOP (40-70 mmHg) and a mid-dilated, non-reactive pupil. Gonioscopy and tonometry separate the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Open-angle glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic, painless condition with a normal-appearing open anterior chamber angle; elevated IOP and optic nerve damage develop over years without acute symptoms. Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents suddenly with severe pain, corneal oedema, and a closed angle. The two conditions have different mechanisms, presentations, and urgency levels.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute conjunctivitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute conjunctivitis causes a red, discharging eye without pain deep in the globe, without haloes, and with a normal intraocular pressure and clear cornea. Acute angle-closure produces a characteristically steamy cornea, fixed mid-dilated pupil, very high IOP, and nausea — features absent in conjunctivitis.

relatedConditions.name: Open-angle glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of glaucoma; shares optic nerve damage mechanisms and treatment drugs but has a fundamentally different angle anatomy and presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Advanced diabetic retinopathy causes neovascular glaucoma — a secondary form of angle closure from rubeosis iridis — and is a key risk condition in the differential.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Lens enlargement from cataract formation contributes to angle narrowing and phacomorphic acute angle closure; cataract extraction is also a definitive treatment option.

relatedConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in older patients and both conditions involve age-related ocular vascular disease; bevacizumab injections for AMD can rarely trigger IOP spikes.

faqs.question: What is acute angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Acute angle-closure glaucoma is a sudden, painful rise in eye pressure caused by the iris blocking the eye's drainage channel. Pressure typically rises from a normal 10-21 mmHg to 40-70 mmHg within hours. It is a sight-threatening emergency requiring immediate treatment to prevent permanent blindness.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of an acute glaucoma attack?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, coloured haloes around lights, a red eye, a fixed mid-dilated pupil, nausea, and vomiting. The combination of headache, eye pain, and nausea is a classic triad that should prompt immediate emergency eye examination, not a gastrointestinal workup.

faqs.question: Is acute angle-closure glaucoma an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intraocular pressure above 40 mmHg destroys retinal ganglion cells irreversibly within hours. Any patient with a red, painful eye, blurred vision, and haloes around lights must attend an emergency eye unit the same day. Delay by even a few hours significantly increases the risk of permanent vision loss.

faqs.question: How is acute angle-closure glaucoma treated?
faqs.answer: Emergency medical treatment uses topical timolol, apraclonidine, and systemic acetazolamide to lower intraocular pressure rapidly. IV mannitol is added for very high pressures. Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy, which creates a hole in the iris to eliminate pupillary block. The fellow eye receives prophylactic iridotomy within days.

faqs.question: Can acute angle-closure glaucoma cause permanent blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes, if untreated or treated too late. The duration and peak of elevated intraocular pressure determines optic nerve damage. Attacks lasting under 24 hours with prompt treatment usually cause minimal permanent loss. Attacks persisting for days can cause complete blindness in the affected eye.

faqs.question: What is laser peripheral iridotomy?
faqs.answer: Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) uses a focused Nd:YAG laser to create a tiny hole in the peripheral iris, allowing aqueous humour to bypass the iris-lens pupillary block. The procedure takes minutes under topical anaesthesia, relieves the angle-closure mechanism in over 90% of primary cases, and is also performed prophylactically in the fellow eye.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Highest-risk groups are East Asian women over age 55, people with short (hyperopic, far-sighted) eyes, anyone with a first-degree relative affected by acute angle closure, and patients prescribed anticholinergic medications such as tricyclic antidepressants or antihistamines. Inuit populations have the highest known global prevalence.

faqs.question: Can medications cause acute angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anticholinergic drugs (tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, inhaled anticholinergics) and sympathomimetics dilate the pupil and can trigger acute closure in anatomically predisposed eyes. Topiramate and other sulfonamides can cause drug-induced angle closure by a different mechanism involving choroidal effusion.

faqs.question: Does the other eye also need treatment after an acute attack?
faqs.answer: Yes. The fellow (unaffected) eye has a greater than 50% lifetime risk of an acute attack because it shares the same anatomical predisposition. Prophylactic laser peripheral iridotomy in the fellow eye reduces this risk to under 5% and should be performed within days of the acute episode.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after acute angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Corneal oedema and eye pain typically resolve within 24-48 hours of IOP normalisation. Visual acuity returns to near-baseline within 1-3 days in uncomplicated cases. Laser iridotomy is performed within 24-48 hours after pressure is controlled. Long-term monitoring for chronic angle-closure glaucoma continues indefinitely.

faqs.question: What is the difference between open-angle and angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Open-angle glaucoma is chronic and painless, with a normal-looking drainage angle but impaired outflow through the trabecular meshwork. Angle-closure glaucoma occurs when the iris physically blocks the drainage angle. Acute angle closure causes sudden severe pain, corneal oedema, and vision loss — symptoms never seen in open-angle glaucoma.

faqs.question: Can cataract surgery prevent acute angle-closure glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Removing the crystalline lens deepens the anterior chamber by 1-2 mm and widens the drainage angle substantially. The EAGLE trial showed lens extraction was superior to laser iridotomy for IOP control in patients with primary angle closure and elevated IOP. In eyes where the lens causes angle narrowing, cataract surgery is both preventive and curative.

faqs.question: Are haloes around lights always a sign of acute glaucoma?
faqs.answer: Not always, but haloes around lights combined with eye pain and redness are a strong warning sign of acute angle-closure glaucoma. Haloes without pain may be caused by corneal dystrophy, cataracts, or normal glare from bright lights. The combination of haloes with pain, reduced vision, and nausea is the classic triad requiring emergency evaluation.

faqs.question: Can acute angle-closure glaucoma recur after laser iridotomy?
faqs.answer: Primary pupillary-block angle closure recurs in under 5% of eyes after a successful iridotomy. However, approximately 30-40% of patients develop chronic angle-closure glaucoma from residual peripheral anterior synechiae formed during the acute attack, requiring ongoing medical or surgical IOP management despite a patent iridotomy.

faqs.question: Is acute angle-closure glaucoma hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a genetic component. First-degree relatives of affected patients have 2-5 times the normal risk of narrow angles and acute angle closure. The heritability is polygenic, linked to genes controlling anterior segment development including axial eye length and anterior chamber depth. Relatives should have an ophthalmological examination with gonioscopy.

metaDescription: Acute angle-closure glaucoma: a sudden, painful rise in eye pressure that threatens sight. Symptoms, emergency diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
keywords: angle closure vs open angle glaucoma
